skill.name: when-using-flow-nexus-platform-use-flow-nexus-platform
skill.description: Manage Flow Nexus authentication, sandboxes, storage, databases, deployments, monitoring, billing, and operational documentation through a structured platform SOP.
skill.summary: Run structured Flow Nexus platform setup, deployment, operations, and billing workflows.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external commands, network use, environment access, and credential references are largely true positives because the skill is an operational SOP that guides platform deployment and billing actions. Weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and the critical combined-heuristic finding are not confirmed as malicious; no prompt injection evidence was found. Publication should require human review, permission gating, and clear warnings before execution.

high_findings.title: High-Privilege Platform and Billing Automation
high_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill guides deployment, monitoring, billing, and auto-refill actions for a live platform, including payment method prerequisites and a configured auto-refill amount. This is dangerous because an assistant could trigger paid operations or alter production resources without strong user confirmation. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The relevant lines explicitly describe payment requirements, billing management, and an enabled auto-refill action; the intent is operational, not merely descriptive.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines explicitly describe payment requirements, billing management, and an enabled auto-refill action. The skill purpose is legitimate, but the operational and financial impact is high.

high_findings.title: Credential and Secret Handling in Deployment Flow
high_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The SOP passes an Anthropic API key and database URL into deployment variables and documents credential storage in memory. This is dangerous because secrets may be copied into prompts, logs, memory stores, or remote deployment contexts. Confidence: 0.84. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines explicitly name ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, DATABASE_URL, credentials, authentication tokens, and memory storage.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly name API keys, database URLs, credentials, tokens, and memory storage. Legitimate platform setup is possible, but secret exposure risk is clear.

medium_findings.title: Generated Scripts and Command Execution
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill instructs users to run npx commands, create shell scripts, and generate JavaScript with child_process imports. This is dangerous because generated scripts may later execute commands in local or sandboxed environments. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The command and child_process patterns are directly present, but many commands are normal for a DevOps SOP.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and child_process patterns are directly present. They appear aligned with the skill purpose, so the risk is elevated but not proof of abuse.

medium_findings.title: Remote Networked Deployment and File Upload Operations
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The SOP connects to Flow Nexus services, configures remote sandboxes, deploys templates, and uploads application content. This is dangerous because it may move user code and configuration to an external service. Confidence: 0.80. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines contain a hardcoded platform URL and deployment or upload actions, but no hidden exfiltration endpoint was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain a hardcoded platform URL and deployment or upload actions. They match the stated platform use case, but the external transfer risk remains real.

medium_findings.title: Stateful Memory Use for Operational Identifiers
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill stores user IDs, sandbox IDs, deployment IDs, monitoring data, balances, and workflow status in Claude Flow memory. This is dangerous because operational identifiers can persist beyond the immediate task and may expose account context. Confidence: 0.76. Confidence reasoning: PROCESS.md and SKILL.md explicitly describe persistent memory keys and stored operational values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly describe persistent memory keys and stored operational values. This is likely intended coordination, but it creates privacy and persistence concerns.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts appear to come from documentation text, Markdown syntax, graph labels, UUID defaults, or unrelated words rather than actual cryptographic function calls. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4 being used for security. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: Reviewed cited regions show prose, diagrams, SQL defaults, or metadata, not cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed cited regions show prose, diagrams, SQL defaults, or metadata. No cryptographic implementation was present in the examined context.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Operational Health Checks
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. System and network reconnaissance alerts map to platform status checks, health checks, logs, and architecture documentation. These are expected for a platform operations skill, although they still require user approval before running. Confidence: 0.83. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines focus on declared Flow Nexus resources and do not instruct broad host or network scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines focus on declared Flow Nexus resources and operational status. They do not instruct broad host discovery or network scanning.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for prompt injection risk. No evidence found in SKILL.md, PROCESS.md, README.md, or process-diagram.gv of instruction override language, claims of pre-approval, or requests to skip security review. Confidence: 0.87. Confidence reasoning: Targeted searches for common injection phrases returned no matches across the files listed in static analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted searches for common injection phrases returned no matches across the files listed in static analysis. This does not eliminate all possible social engineering, but no evidence was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Variables Passed to Deployment
dangerous_patterns.description: The deployment example includes ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and DATABASE_URL values. These must be handled only with explicit user consent and secure secret storage. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The variable names are directly present in the deployment block.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variable names are directly present in the deployment block. The pattern is a known source of accidental secret disclosure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated child_process Import
dangerous_patterns.description: The generated initialization script imports child_process and promisifies exec. This can become command execution if later logic adds dynamic commands. Confidence: 0.72. Confidence reasoning: The import is explicit, but the nearby snippet does not show an executed dynamic command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The import is explicit, but the nearby snippet does not show an executed dynamic command. Risk depends on future generated code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution from Persisted Memory
dangerous_patterns.description: Several commands retrieve IDs from persistent memory using shell substitution. If stored values are untrusted, later shell use may create injection risk. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: The shell substitution pattern is explicit, while exploitation would require malicious stored values or unsafe later use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell substitution pattern is explicit. Exploitation would require malicious stored values or unsafe later command construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Billing Refill
dangerous_patterns.description: The SOP configures auto-refill with enabled set to true and an amount of 50. This can create financial impact if executed without approval. Confidence: 0.89. Confidence reasoning: The billing action and amount are explicit and operational.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The billing action and amount are explicit and operational. It is a legitimate feature with high consequence if automated.

content.value_statement: Flow Nexus setup can span authentication, sandboxes, storage, databases, deployment, monitoring, and billing. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured SOP for coordinating those platform tasks.
actual_capabilities: Guides Flow Nexus authentication, registration, login, and profile verification workflows.
Coordinates sandbox creation, storage setup, database schema creation, and real-time subscriptions.
Provides deployment steps for templates and custom applications on Flow Nexus.
Documents monitoring, logs, health checks, incident response, and operations utilities.
Explains billing checks, payment history review, credit balance tracking, and auto-refill setup.
limitations: Requires an installed Flow Nexus MCP server and valid platform access.
Includes commands that can affect remote resources, billing, and deployments.
Uses placeholder credentials and example values that must be replaced securely.
Does not independently verify Flow Nexus pricing, availability, or account permissions.

use_cases.title: Set Up a New Platform Workspace
use_cases.description: Create an authenticated Flow Nexus environment with initial service configuration, memory checkpoints, and documentation.

use_cases.title: Deploy and Monitor an Application
use_cases.description: Use templates or custom scripts to deploy an application, collect logs, and check operational health.

use_cases.title: Review Billing and Usage Operations
use_cases.description: Check balances, review payment history, and document credit management practices before enabling automation.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Setup Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: First Flow Nexus setup with minimal automation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Flow Nexus platform skill to create a setup checklist. Do not run commands. Include prerequisites, account steps, and approval points.

prompt_templates.title: Service Configuration Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan sandboxes, storage, database, and subscriptions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Flow Nexus platform skill to plan service configuration for a development workspace. Identify each command that needs user approval.

prompt_templates.title: Deployment Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Flow Nexus platform skill to draft a deployment runbook for a Node application. Keep secrets out of logs and mark manual confirmation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Operations Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Audit a full operational workflow before execution.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Flow Nexus platform skill to review authentication, deployment, monitoring, and billing steps. Flag risky commands and propose safer alternatives.

output_examples.input: Plan a Flow Nexus setup for a new development workspace.
output: Authentication, sandbox, storage, and database steps grouped by approval stage.
Secrets and billing actions marked for manual handling.

output_examples.input: Prepare an application deployment runbook for Flow Nexus.
output: Deployment path selected with template and custom options.
Health checks, logs, rollback notes, and monitoring checkpoints listed.
Environment variables referenced without exposing secret values.

output_examples.input: Review billing automation before enabling auto-refill.
output: Current balance and payment history checks separated from write actions.
Auto-refill threshold and amount require explicit confirmation.
Cost controls and owner approval are documented.

best_practices: Require explicit approval before running commands that deploy, upload, configure billing, or change remote resources.
Use a secret manager for API keys, database URLs, tokens, and payment-related configuration.
Start with dry-run planning, then execute one phase at a time with logged approvals.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real passwords, API keys, database URLs, or payment details into prompts.
Do not enable auto-refill or paid resource scaling without a named owner approval.
Do not run generated shell scripts before reviewing their commands and target environment.

faq.question: Does this skill run Flow Nexus commands by itself?
faq.answer: The skill provides command-oriented procedures. The assistant should request approval before executing any command or write action.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production environments?
faq.answer: It needs careful review before production use because it covers deployments, monitoring, secrets, and billing operations.

faq.question: Can it manage billing tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes balance checks, payment history review, and auto-refill setup, so billing steps need explicit confirmation.

faq.question: What tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects Flow Nexus MCP access, Claude Flow hooks, network connectivity, and relevant platform credentials.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use secure environment management or a secret manager. Do not place real secret values in chat, generated files, or logs.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace audit high risk?
faq.answer: The skill combines command execution, network deployment, environment secrets, persistent memory, and payment automation.
